item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article was taken by the author for this article, and as such is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Sounds good! Feel free to add that one yourself in your preferred location, you'll save me some paper work and you some time :P

item.title: Re: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Looks good to me, maybe add in another link to that one cardboard dog SCP esko wrote.

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edits
- hide

To link to other content featuring entities mimicking dead celebrities, I propose the following edit:

Human subjects exposed to an instance of SCP-1901-1 without being informed of its true nature will perceive it as a human being identical to the person depicted.1

Footnotes
1. Due to both anomalies involving the emulation of celebrities, a possible link between this phenomenon and SCP-2137 is under investigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When affected in this way, manifestations of SCP-1901-2 display sociopathic tendencies and total recall of the subject's memory.

What sociopathic tendencies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was joking, people. That wasn't what was removed at all (I saw an early draft with the now-removed bit). But this is an awesome idea, hmm?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description is a bit convoluted, a bit too much jumping back and forth between mentions of -1 and -2 for my taste.
I also missed the reference in the letter, probably because I'm no music buff. I could tell there was one, but I just didn't get it.

On the flip-side, the concept is interesting and I see no tone or grammar breaks. I think I would have enjoyed it if I was closer to the target audience; as it is, not so much. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mainly because of the "Elvis Has Left the Planet" trope. Alien Elvis is overdone.

That said, I think it sounds pretty bitchin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would people hate on that? It sounds awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's what I thought as well.
Fo those of you who haven't seen it, there's a documentary called "Paul Mcartney Really Is Dead." It's not a true story, but it was interesting to watch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That Elvis is actually an alien/human hybrid that didn't die, but rather was retrieved by his people in order to spread the power of rock-n'-roll to the stars. They were gracious enough to leave a meat-clone behind, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm remaining neutral on this one. For reasons a few people have already stated, this just doesn't do much to interest me. I don't necessarily dislike it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Celebrities are cardboard cutouts that crave attention.

Biting satire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The document was trying to imply that these are earlier models, and that better/different versions exist

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very interesting, and written quite well.
However, I think the whole 'Paul is Dead' thing does not tie up with the traits of -1 and -2. -1 can be easily seen through, and -2 always tries to find a 'hot spot'.
What I really wanted to see when I read halfway was that upon -2 going into the 'hot spot', -2 would slowly become the actual human, or at least, the allusion would extend to all humans, regardless of the requirements listed in the article.
Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a minute to figure out the difference between the -1 and -2s, but I was happy when I did. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wake up in the morning and the first thing I see is the "Most Recently Created" page. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The letter at the end is a reference to the "Paul is Dead" rumor, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cracked a smile at the Elvis bit. Take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brief and intresting.

But there's still something missing here….
Withholding vote for now.

Edit: I see what you did there… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found the idea interesting enough to warrant an upvote, but overall I didn't find this too compelling. That being said, can people say no to -2 instances, or are they being compelled to do so? And if they don't, what does the instance do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not interested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What was it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Filling in the spaces for redacted items makes me feel clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: everybody else hated it :x

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say that the Foundation wouldn't be pansy-ass about censorship. Either something is important enough to not let people know, or it's okay for them to know. I can't think of a good reason they would actively put in more effort to give the readers a clue in-universe.

But it's not affecting my vote either way, and I'm probably the only one who cares, so take it as you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You took out the good part. :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way I see it…

When it comes to using black boxes in these documents, in-universe, sometimes it's less about full censorship and more about plausible deniability. Sure, Elvis Presley is the name likely under █████ ███████, but it could be Frank Sinatra, you can't know for sure, the point is for the person reading to draw a reasonable conclusion that a well-known, dead musician is being discussed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Oh hey, Rhonda. Next time you use the marker for blacking out people's names, make sure you leave the spaces in."

"…why? I mean, if I'm supposed to be hiding the information, does it really make sense for me to lift up the mark-"

"GETTING REAL TIRED OF YOUR SHIT RHONDA."

My point is that leaving the space in "Elvis Presley" is stupidious.

On the whole, though, I'm gonna no-vote this. It's too interesting to downvote, but it doesn't do enough for me to upvote either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You clever bastard.

+1
